document_index.title: Project’s meeting notes
document_index.excerpt: Participants: Tuulia Valius, Edward Novikov, Tanjina Kader, Fernando Perez, Anniina Farkkila For this project, we will start by using the sciset dataset, which consists of 22 samples: 6 pairs before and after neoadjuvant chemotherapy. The chemotherapy used was platinum and taxane-based. The samples correspond to whole slide images (WSI) that were analysed with tCycif using 25 antibodies. There are several tumor microenvironment (TME) biomarkers associated with chemotherapy treatment detected during the sciset project. Some of the TME biomarkers include: Immunosuppressive myeloid areas interacting with CD8 T-cells Immunosuppression in the EMT tumor-stromal interface? Enrichment of fibroblast Anni Virtanen, a pathologist, will select regions of interest from the WSI to create synthetic TMAs.

The quantifications are done with each combination of possible parameters to study the effects of each parameter. Project’s meeting notes
June 8th 2023
Participants: Tuulia Valius, Edward Novikov, Tanjina Kader, Fernando Perez, Anniina Farkkila
For this project, we will start by using the sciset dataset, which consists of 22 samples: 6 pairs before and after neoadjuvant chemotherapy. The chemotherapy used was platinum and taxane-based.
The samples correspond to whole slide images (WSI) that were analysed with tCycif using 25 antibodies. There are several tumor microenvironment (TME) biomarkers associated with chemotherapy treatment detected during the sciset project. Some of the TME biomarkers include:
Immunosuppressive myeloid areas interacting with CD8 T-cells
Immunosuppression in the EMT tumor-stromal interface?
Enrichment of fibroblast
Enrichment of T-cells
Others...
Anni Virtanen, a pathologist, will select regions of interest from the WSI to create synthetic TMAs. 
Edward highlighted a type of spatial-power analysis for channel correlation in the WSI, to validate the performance of TMAs. But after discussion, we decided to focus on the biomarkers that have already been detected. Anniina mentioned that also the cell-to-cell fractions of neighbors could be used to validate the performance of TMAs.
The synthetic TMAs might be created by the pathologist to recapture some of the TME biomarkers detected from the WSI. We need to define which TME biomarkers will be used as the ground truth. Tuulia noted that the pathologist should first explore the data and determine if it is possible to select regions that could detect those biomarkers.
After the pathologist selects the regions, we will explore how many cores are needed and from which regions
Inga-Maria, Anniina, and Fernando first will establish the ground truth, so Anni could know which areas to select.

July 5th 2023
Attendees Anniina, Inga-Maria, Anni, and Fernando.
As materials for the project, we will include 26 whole slide images from the Topacio dataset plus 21 samples from the Sciset dataset to have in total 47 whole slide images.
Then using the two combined datasets we will explore differences in cell type proportions and cell neighbourhoods in the samples before NACT vs samples exposed to NACT. With this exploration, we will expand our ground truth.
There are three approaches that we will explore:
Selection of cores by pathologist from H&E whole slide images.
Exploration of different sets of cores (see below).
Selection of cores by a semi-random approach.
Using computational tools we will detect cancer rich and stromal rich areas from the H&E or Fluorescence microscopy images (to be defined). And then using computational tools we will select random cores from the cancer and stromal rich areas.
Selection of cores by a random approach.
Randomly using computational tools we will select cores from the whole slide images.
For each of the approaches we will explore a different number of cores, core sizes and different combinations of sets of cores selected.
For the selection of cores by pathologist approach:
Anni will select four types of cores from the H&E whole slide images:
Cancer rich
Stromal rich
Lymphocyte immune rich
Cancer-stroma interface

For the integration of the H&E annotations with the fluorescence microscopy images, Fernando is working in a systematic way to do this in QuPath.
For the selection of in a semi-random approach
Fernando will explore with Tuulia, Sandro from LSP a way to computationally detect cancer-rich and stromal-rich areas.
For all the approaches
Fernando will be working on the integration of the Topacio dataset to get new ground truth results. And he is working on the creation of a pipeline to perform all analysis.

The literature review made by Annti was great. We will stop this and continue with the improvement of the analysis pipeline.

We will also focus on power analysis, based on a ground truth of samples.

For slide1:
Next, I’m going to present what are our current approaches and preliminary results
For slide2:
In this image we are observing a high grade serous cancer tumor section coloured by the intensity of 6 markers.
We can observe that the TME is heterogeneous, as we already know. If we do not have a prior hypothesis, then, aiming to recapture its complexity by randomly sampling one or two cores, as those highlighted in red circles, could be too optimistic or counterintuitive. 
But, what if we focus on patterns we are suspicious of that can distinguish two groups of samples instead of the whole TME heterogeneity?
For slide3:
For example, in samples with different molecular profiles or chemotherapy exposure status we can observe different patterns. For example, samples extracted before chemotherapy have a higher higher stromal rich areas, as the one seen in the left, whereas samples after chemotherapy exposure have higher myeloid-derived immune cell infiltration.
Then, if we sample from these two groups of samples and if the N is large enough, we could still recapture the TME differences among these two groups. 
Taking this in mind, we have two main questions in our study.
For slide4:
If we are using cohorts (a large N), can we recapture TME features, tentative biomarkers, using tissue microarrays?
And, is there a strategy of TMA construction that increases the concordance to whole slide imaging?
For example, a selection of cores guided by a pathologist instead of just random sampling?
How many cores are needed?
For slide5:
During my summer internship in 2023 in the Peter Sorger’s Lab I address part of these questions and I will give a short summary.
For slide6:
For materials we used the tCycIF images from the project that was published in cancer cell
For slide7:

ide5:

For slide6:
For materials we used the tCycIF images from the project that was published in cancer cell
For slide7:
First we labeled cancer rich areas. In the squares.
Then computationally we sample the cores from those areas according to some parameters as for example. If we want that the cores have the maximum possible distance between them. The minimum number of cells per core. If the cores overlap or not, and radius of core and number of cores per sample.
Also we performed random core selection.
For slide8:
We got some interesting results
For slide9:
Here in red are cores from cancer rich areas selected by humans. 
In the boxplots, every dot represents each of those four cores.
And the red start is the value observed in whole slide images.
What we see is that the median proportion on the four cores, of different immune cells is not so far from the ground true value in WSI.
For slide9:
And also really interesting is that if we explore the cell neighbourhoods we find more similarities. In the first row if the frequency that Epithelial cells have a fibroblast cells in a radius or 30microns or 65 microns or 130microns. It seems that these proportions are very well preserved in our guided TMAs.
For slide10:
But when we use random TMAs, the differences compared to the ground truth changes.

Azzalini et al. 2023: 
Overview of Tumor Heterogeneity in High-Grade Serous Ovarian Cancers
Overview of HGSOC tumor heterogeneity. HGSOC are 70% of epithelial ovarian carcinomas (EOC). Typically found in advanced stages. Characterized by remarkable intratumor heterogeneity, with different morphologies and tissue structures within the same tumor. 96% have TP53 mutations, many of which also have high chromosomal instability (CIN) and homologous recombination deficiency (HRD), which is a biomarker for platinum and PARP-inhibitor therapy. Disease progression, resistance and recurrence are linked to ITH in tumor masses.
H&E morphologies classified into transitional, solid, pseudo-endometroid, papillary, infiltrative micropapillae, and papillary infiltrative, divided into classic papillary and SET groups. Molecular morphologies classified into mesenchymal transition (MT), immune reactive (IR), solid and proliferative (SP), and papilloglandular (PG) types. IR and PG types have better outcomes than MT and SP. Typically multiple morphologies present in one sample.

morphologies classified into mesenchymal transition (MT), immune reactive (IR), solid and proliferative (SP), and papilloglandular (PG) types. IR and PG types have better outcomes than MT and SP. Typically multiple morphologies present in one sample. Literature inaccurate on reported frequencies as often only the prevalent morphology in a sample is considered. Papillary features most widespread. Some morphologies mimic other EOC types. BRCA mutations associated with SET characteristics, high levels of mitotic index and TIL, serous “undifferentiated” morphology, and giant atypical nuclei, along with different metastatic patterns. SET features have better response and outcome over classical features. Heterogeneity of tumor growth patterns measured by Shannon’s index is a prognostic biomarker under neoadjuvant chemotherapy. 
Morphologic features in WSI can predict molecular alterations like BRCA deficiency and MSI-H, as well as prognosis. Positive and negative morphologies can be present in the same sample. TME interactions can be behind heterogeneity. Stromal cells seen as a resource for tumor growth. Shift towards resource-rich TME associated with aggressive cancer behavior. Insufficient evidence to link IHC biomarkers and growth patterns. Morphological and clonal heterogeneity don’t always overlap. Only occasional spatial correspondence between IHC biomarkers and tumor pattern.
Histo-morphologies are correlated with varying transcriptional signatures, which predict prognosis. HM also correlated with pre-operative features, such as MT type more common in late-stage peritoneal tumors with worse progn. Different HM types related to different sets of genes.

Association between HGSOC architecture and molecular and biophysical features, such as tumor stiffness and AKT isoform expression pattern, has been shown. Expression patterns from bulk tissue analysis can be heavily influenced by stromal and immune cells. It’s possible that MT HM mirrors stromal cell expression, while IR tumors are SP or PC tumors with a prominent immune response. Temporal and spatial evolution changes morphology. Three temporal trajectories have been identified: evolving (early), maintaining (intermediate), and adapting (late), each with different typical cell content, genomic and transcriptional features.
Tumors have been classified with preop laparoscopic imaging into two types: (1) deep and invasive appearance with distorted or retracted surrounding tissues, or (2) superficial appearance with exophytic nodules outlined by normal tissue. Both have distinct surgical outcomes and molecular signatures. Histological analysis shows no significant microscopy pattern in the types, although papillary architecture is more common in type 1 (50% vs. 5%). The types also have distinct radiographic features.
Clonal evolution is related to genomic instability as a major feature of the carcinogenic process. HGSOC is genomically unstable, with the underlying biological processes being established early. The genomic variations of each patient are very heterogeneous. p53 loss near ubiquitous, sold tumors 100% but ascites 91% CIN, suggesting a dynamic process.

The genomic variations of each patient are very heterogeneous. p53 loss near ubiquitous, sold tumors 100% but ascites 91% CIN, suggesting a dynamic process. Extensive HRD and CNV driving ITH through dysregulation of apoptosis and DNA repair. Point mutations at tumor suppressors or oncogenes are unusual. Distinct mutational profiles associated with age at diagnosis. Tumor suppressor loss and oncogene amplification mainly due to loss of heterozygosity (LOH), telomeric allelic imbalance (TAI), and large-scale state transitions. PTEN, RB1, NF1, RAD51B breakage are common in HGSOC. CCNE1, MYC, MECOM, and PIK3CA often amplified. 
Quantifying ITH in HGSOC is difficult because it requires inferring phylogenetic trees with unknown parental allele phasing and horizontal dependencies between adjacent genomic loci. Studies of ITH in HGSOC have been focused on spatial and temporal ITH by comparing primary tumors to metastases. No studies multisampling primary tumors, lack of information on the possible mono-/polyclonal origin of the disease. Currently, it’s feasible to consider tumors polyclonal. Spatial ITH likely arises from metastasis to metastasis spread, rather than successive metastases from primary tumor. Clonal evolution from primary cancer to recurrences has been documented spatio-temporally. High clonal expansion associated with drug resistant clones. 
Two evolutionary topologies proposed: star (polyclonal origin, monoclonal spreading), and tree (monoclonal origin, polyclonal spreading). Heterogeneity driven by evolutionary moves increasing genomic instability and subclonal expansion. ITH calls for multi-site biopsies.

Epigenetic regulation is prevalent in clonal evolution of HGSOC. HRD can result from methylation of BRCA promoters. Epigenetic HRD has worse prognosis and Pt-resistance than genetic HRD, comparable to HR type cancers. Targeting hypermethylation could be a treatment opportunity. On the other hand, genome-wide hypomethylation is also associated with cancer features, such as CIN, depression of imprinted genes and retrotransposons, and aberrant expression. There is near universal CpG hypomethylation in HGSOC, Hypomethylation at three distinct chromosomal sites categorises patients with better prognoses over hypermethylated HGSOC. Histone modification can be involved in tumorigenesis and drug resistance, but in the case of H3K14ac being associated with PARPi resistance, its medical depletion didn’t re-sensitize the cancer cells, which suggests that other histone modification -associated proteins are directly involved in the resistance. Upregulation of other histone modifications and involved proteins are also associated with resistance.

MT-heavy tumors are characterized by low genomic alteration, activation of EM-transitional TFs, decreased epithelial markers, increased mesenchymal markers, and diverse cell composition. The EMT index is a measure of these features, and has prognostic value.

Approach:
“Comparison of 0.6 vs. 1.0 mm TMA cores by analytical and technical accuracy of cores against WSI”.
Diagnostic slides from three tissue blocks per patient were assessed by pathologists to identify the target tissues (normal, tumor, and cis). Core placement was chosen by a pathologist for each slide in the blocks. Three cores per tissue block were extracted for the 0.6 mm TMA (9 cores total per patient) and two cores per block for the 1.0 mm TMA (6 total). 
Technical accuracy was evaluated by scoring each core by how much target tissue they captured (Score 3 if target tissue > 50% of core, 2 if < 50%, 1 if no target tissue, and 0 if no tissue at all). Only cores with a score of 2 or 3 were considered usable, and were included in the analysis. Every patient with at least 2 usable cores total was included. The quality of captured tissue was defined as the mean score of all usable cores.

Approach:
The total dataset consisted of 60 TMA cores from 30 patients, WSI from 64 patients, and 3D reconstructed tissue blocks from 7 patients, plus 7 normal controls. Tissue images were segmented into 10 tissue types (pancreatic cancer, pancreatic cancer precursor lesions, normal ductal epithelium, acinar tissue, islets of Langerhans, vasculature, nerves, fat, and stroma) at a 1 µm per pixel scale using a deep learning pipeline. 
Tissue composition in WSI was defined as the area percentage of each type. Variation in tissue composition was defined as the distribution of compositions of each tissue type. 3D variation in tissue composition was defined as the distribution of compositions of each tissue type, taking each serial image in the z-stack as an independent measurement.
vTMA were generated by taking random coordinates from the WSIs or 3D z-stack and taking a 1 mm diameter disk, centered on the coordinates, from the image. The tissue composition of the first virtual core was calculated and compared against the WSI and 3D compositions.

Approach: The cohort was a mix of 174 borderline, invasive and primary peritoneal tumors of epithelial ovarian cancer. TMA cores were extracted from tissue blocks by core needle biopsy, triplicate cores for each patient. Stained cores were manually evaluated by a pathologist and categorized by the percent of positive cells: 0, 1-10,11-25, 26-50, >50 %. The intra- and interpatient variance in patients was estimated using a random effects model and used to calculate the ICC, providing an estimate of the reproducibility across the triplicate cores. ICC was calculated as (inter-patient variance) / (inter-patient variance + intra-patient variance). Natural logarithmic transformation of the stain values was used because it was more normal.

Approach: The discovery cohort consisted of treatment-naive breast tumor tissue blocks from 484 patients. Three sections were taken from each block, one each from the top, middle and bottom of the block, and placed on the same imaging slide. The slides were H&E stained and imaged. PAM50 status was determined by expression microarrays. Tumor cellularity was categorized into low, moderate, or high (< 40 %, 40 > < 70 %, > 70 % tumor DNA). Slide contents were segmented algorithmically. vTMA were constructed by picking the first cell in a list to be a potential core center, checking if there are more than T = 100 cells within the core radius (R=0.3 mm), and then either accepting or rejecting the core. Cores were biopsied from the selected areas for both virtual and real TMAs. The analysis results were compared between TMAs and WSI. Only cores that were at least 50% entire and contained tumor cells were scored. Cores with folds, tears or over 50% loss were not scored. 
The distributions of TMA percentage of positive cells for each marker was summarised by boxplots. TMA intensity was summarised by barcharts. The targeted true value percentage (TVP) and intensity (TVI) were read from the WSI and compared with the TMA estimates.

The targeted true value percentage (TVP) and intensity (TVI) were read from the WSI and compared with the TMA estimates. An exact match was defined as median of the core values being equal to the TVP, and a range match was defined as the TVP falling between the first and third quartiles of the core values. A bootstrap resampling technique without replacement was used to estimate the variability in selecting a few cores per region for the TVP and TVI. 1-10 cores were sampled for 1000 iterations and the mean and sd were recorded for percentage of positive cells. The percentage of each intensity was also recorded. Negative cores were excluded from bootstrapping. 
Accuracy was defined as the TVP captured by the 95% bootstrap CI. Precision was defined by the width of the 95% bootstrap CI. Both were evaluated for each marker by the number of cores sampled for each case. A sufficient number of cores needed to capture the TVP was deemed achieved when the sampled cores from both the virtual and real TMAs’ bootstrap CI captured the TVP and the real TMA 95% CI lower bound was greater than 0. The optimal number of cores per sample was deemed as the lowest sufficient number of cores. The median and mode of the optimal number of cores for each marker was used to determine the recommended number of cores to sample.

The pan-cytokeratin stained WSI were annotated for the complete tumor and the border zone tumor-surrounding tissue ROI. For vTMA construction, a grid with a grid point distance of twice the core radius was superimposed on the WSI. Thus, every grid point corresponded to the center of a vTMA core. All cores within the annotated ROI were saved. Batch effects resulting from different times of staining between the WSI and real TMA were first corrected with Rheinard stain normalisation. Approach: Post-mortem tissue WSIs of brain white matter lesions were stained with microglial markers Iba-1, CD68 and MHCII IHC. The specimens contain three groups: Control (nonlesional white matter from a brain in which no white matter lesions were present); deep subcortical lesions (DSCL); normal-appearing white matter (NAWM; white matter that did not appear to be lesional on MRI or to the naked eye but from a brain where DSCL where present elsewhere).

The features assessed for each ROI were: Area density = (total area of microglial labels) / (total area of all labels) * 100%. Average microglial size = (Total area of microglial markers) / (number of microglial markers). Microglial shape metric = Ci * [(Ai - Ao) / Ao), where Ci = (4*π*Ai / p^2). Ci is the profile’s roundness (proximity to true circle), Ai is the profile’s area, Ao is the cut-off value (26.45μm2) and P is the profile’s perimeter length. 
Five cases from each group were assessed. One section per marker was stained and analysed from each case. These included regions representing the total white matter within that section (the whole ROI). In the control and NAWM sections this ROI consisted of the entirety of the white matter, apart from that immediately underlying to the cortex or at the periphery of the section (allowing 12mm clearance). For the DSCL sections this ROI incorporated the extent of microglial activation and alteration to an amoeboid phenotype. 
From the ROI, three 1.75 mm virtual cores were picked. The core size was picked to simulate a 2.0 mm real core with unanalysable edge artifacting.

From the ROI, three 1.75 mm virtual cores were picked. The core size was picked to simulate a 2.0 mm real core with unanalysable edge artifacting. Means of two, three and one cores were calculated, with cores randomly assigned to be included. Correlation between core-based and the whole ROI measurements was assessed with Spearman’s rank correlation. Kolmogorov-Smirnov goodness of fit test (KS) indicated that many data sets deviated from a normal distribution. Bland-Altman plots were also used to assess agreement between TMA and the whole ROI. As many of the datasets weren’t normally distributed, analysis was based on defining acceptable error ranges. These were calculated from the range of the whole ROI measurement ± the accepted error, with clear outliers removed. 
Accuracy was assessed by counting the proportion of virtual core measurements within the acceptable error range. The accuracies were interpreted in light of intergroup differences among the means of the ROIs.
